text.title: Python Async Patterns — Core Rules
text.summary: Testable rules every python-async application must obey.

rule.statement: Use asyncio.TaskGroup for fan-out where all subtasks must succeed or none. Use gather only when you need ordered partial-failure semantics with return_exceptions=True.
rule.rationale: TaskGroup cancels siblings on first failure; gather leaves orphans unless return_exceptions is set — and that requires manual error inspection per result.

rule.statement: Use asyncio.Semaphore to cap concurrent I/O. Acquire the semaphore inside the task coroutine, not outside it.
rule.rationale: Inside-acquire lets all tasks be created cheaply while limiting concurrent execution; outside-acquire serialises task creation and defeats the purpose.

rule.statement: Use asyncio.timeout(seconds) (3.11+) for per-call deadlines. Do not use asyncio.wait_for for new code.
rule.rationale: asyncio.timeout is a context manager that can be extended or rescheduled; it raises TimeoutError that integrates with standard exception handling.

rule.statement: Never call time.sleep, sync requests, or sync DB drivers inside async def. Use asyncio.sleep, httpx.AsyncClient, asyncpg/aiosqlite respectively.
rule.rationale: A single blocking call stalls the entire event loop and freezes every other coroutine until it returns.

rule.statement: Always store the return value of asyncio.create_task in a variable or collection. Garbage-collected tasks raise 'Task was destroyed but it is pending'.
rule.rationale: The event loop holds only a weak reference; without a strong ref the task can be collected mid-flight.
